verification.rationale: [common_knowledge] The early 19th century saw mass Bible distribution through the great Bible societies (British and Foreign Bible Society, 1804; American Bible Society, 1816), a textbook-level fact.
verification.modern_consensus: The Bible-society movement drove wide Bible circulation from the early 1800s.

claims.quote: was not followed by a corresponding advance in knowledge of revealed truth, or in experimental religion.
claims.claim_text: The wide circulation of the Bible in the early nineteenth century was not followed by a corresponding advance in knowledge of revealed truth or in experimental religion.

verification.rationale: Evaluative judgment about spiritual advance; not empirically testable.

claims.quote: Satan could not, as in former ages, keep God’s word from the people; it had been placed within the reach of all; but in order still to accomplish his object, he led many to value it but lightly.
claims.claim_text: Unable to keep God's word from the people once the Bible was widely accessible, Satan instead led many to value it lightly.

verification.rationale: Theological claim invoking Satanic agency; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: Men neglected to search the Scriptures, and thus they continued to accept false interpretations, and to cherish doctrines which had no foundation in the Bible.
claims.claim_text: People neglected to search the Scriptures and consequently continued to accept false interpretations and doctrines with no biblical foundation.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/theological generalization about neglect of Scripture and 'false' doctrines; not empirically testable.

claims.quote: Seeing the failure of his efforts to crush out the truth by persecution, Satan had again resorted to the plan of compromise which led to the great apostasy and the formation of the Church of Rome.
claims.claim_text: After persecution failed to crush the truth, Satan resorted again to the plan of compromise that had earlier led to the great apostasy and the formation of the Roman Catholic Church.

verification.rationale: Theological claim about Satanic strategy and the 'great apostasy'; unverifiable by definition.

claims.quote: He had induced Christians to ally themselves, not now with pagans, but with those who, by their devotion to the things of this world, had proved themselves to be as truly idolaters as were the worshipers of graven images.
claims.claim_text: Satan induced Christians to ally themselves with worldly people who, through devotion to worldly things, were as truly idolaters as worshipers of graven images.

verification.rationale: Theological claim invoking Satanic agency and defining worldliness as idolatry; unverifiable.

claims.quote: And the results of this union were no less pernicious now than in former ages; pride and extravagance were fostered under the guise of religion, and the churches became corrupted.
claims.claim_text: The union of the church with worldly people produced pernicious results, fostering pride and extravagance under the guise of religion and corrupting the churches.

verification.rationale: Evaluative theological judgment about church corruption; not empirically testable.

claims.quote: Satan continued to pervert the doctrines of the Bible, and traditions that were to ruin millions were taking deep root.
claims.claim_text: Satan continued to pervert biblical doctrines, and traditions that would ruin millions of people took deep root.

verification.rationale: Theological claim invoking Satanic agency and eschatological consequence; unverifiable.

claims.quote: The church was upholding and defending these traditions, instead of contending for “the faith which was once delivered to the saints.”
claims.claim_text: The Protestant church upheld and defended corrupted traditions instead of contending for 'the faith which was once delivered to the saints.'
claims.extractor_note: Contains an embedded biblical allusion (Jude 1:3, 'the faith which was once delivered unto the saints') presented without direct attribution in the chapter text.

verification.rationale: Interpretive/theological judgment about the church defending tradition over the biblical 'faith once delivered' (Jude 3); the embedded scriptural allusion is accurate, but the surrounding proposition is an evaluative theological claim, not empirically testable.

claims.quote: Thus were degraded the principles for which the Reformers had done and suffered so much.
claims.claim_text: The principles for which the Protestant Reformers had labored and suffered were ultimately degraded.

verification.rationale: Evaluative concluding judgment about the degradation of Reformation principles; not empirically testable.
